title: Autoimmune Polyendocrine Syndromes
authors: Peter A. Gottlieb, Aaron W. Michels

items.title: Classification and Clinical Relevance of APS Types
items.content: Autoimmune polyendocrine syndromes (APS) are categorized into two primary types: APS-1 and APS-2. While some groups further subdivide APS-2 into APS-3 and APS-4 based on the type of autoimmunity, this additional classification does not significantly enhance understanding of disease pathogenesis or prevention of complications.
keyPoints: APS is divided into APS-1 and APS-2
Further subdivision into APS-3 and APS-4 is noted but not clinically clarifying
items.reference: Autoimmune Polyendocrine Syndromes · Autoimmune Polyendocrine Syndromes

items.title: Importance of Recognizing Nonendocrine Disease Associations in APS
items.content: APS syndromes, while primarily involving endocrine targets due to autoimmune disorders, also include associations with nonendocrine diseases. This highlights the importance of recognizing these broader disease manifestations for early diagnosis and treatment in patients and their families.
keyPoints: APS involves both endocrine and nonendocrine disease associations
Recognition leads to early diagnosis and treatment
items.reference: Autoimmune Polyendocrine Syndromes · Autoimmune Polyendocrine Syndromes

items.title: Understanding APS-1 Pathogenesis
items.content: APS-1 is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by mutations in the AIRE gene, leading to decreased expression of tissue-specific self-antigens. This allows autoreactive T cells to avoid central deletion during thymic maturation.
keyPoints: Caused by mutations in the AIRE gene on chromosome 21
>100 mutations described
Higher frequency within certain ethnic groups (e.g., Iranian Jews, Sardinians)
Nonclassical form has autosomal dominant mutations primarily in the PHD1 domain

items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Progression of APS-1
items.content: Classical APS-1 often begins with chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis, followed by hypoparathyroidism and adrenal insufficiency. The time between manifestations can span years.
keyPoints: Chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis is the first manifestation
>85% of cases develop hypoparathyroidism
Nearly 80% have Addison's disease
Gonadal failure more common in women (70%) than men (25%)
77% experience dental enamel hypoplasia

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of APS-1
items.content: Diagnosis is clinical when two major components are present. Genetic analysis of the AIRE gene confirms mutations, and anti-interferon antibodies can identify nearly all cases.
keyPoints: Diagnosed clinically with two of three major disorders
Genetic analysis identifies AIRE gene mutations
Detection of anti-interferon α and ω antibodies identifies ~100% of cases
Siblings may be affected even if only one component is detected

items.title: Importance of Autoantibody Assays in APS-1 Diagnosis and Risk Assessment
items.content: Autoantibody assays are crucial for confirming diagnoses and assessing future disease risks in APS-1. Specific antibodies, such as anticytokine antibodies to IL-17 and IL-22, confirm mucocutaneous candidiasis, while anti-21-hydroxylase or anti-17-hydroxylase antibodies indicate Addison's disease risk.
keyPoints: Detection of specific autoantibodies confirms diagnoses (e.g., anticytokine antibodies to IL-17 and IL-22 for mucocutaneous candidiasis).
Anti-21-hydroxylase or anti-17-hydroxylase antibodies confirm Addison's disease risk.
Regular screening for other autoantibodies related to type 1 diabetes, pernicious anemia, etc., every 6-12 months.

items.title: Management of Adrenal Insufficiency in APS-1 Patients with Hypothyroidism
items.content: In APS-1, adrenal insufficiency can be masked by hypothyroidism, necessitating careful management. Thyroid hormone replacement should only follow glucocorticoid treatment to prevent an adrenal crisis.
keyPoints: Adrenal insufficiency may be masked by primary hypothyroidism due to prolonged cortisol half-life.
Thyroid hormone replacement can precipitate adrenal crisis if not preceded by glucocorticoid treatment.
Screen all patients with hypothyroidism and potential APS for adrenal insufficiency before starting thyroid hormone therapy.

items.title: Characteristics and Prevalence of APS-2
items.content: APS-2 is more prevalent than APS-1, affecting 1–2 in 100,000 individuals. It has a gender bias with a female-to-male ratio of at least 3:1. The onset typically occurs in adulthood, peaking between ages 20 and 60.
keyPoints: Prevalence: 1–2 in 100,000
Gender bias: Female to male ratio ≥ 3:1
Onset age peak: 20-60 years

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria for APS-2
items.content: APS-2 is diagnosed by the presence of two or more specific endocrine deficiencies. These include primary adrenal insufficiency, Graves' disease/autoimmune thyroiditis, type 1 diabetes mellitus, and primary hypogonadism.
keyPoints: Primary adrenal insufficiency: 50–70%
Graves' disease/autoimmune thyroiditis: 15–69%
Type 1 diabetes mellitus (T1D): 40–50%
Primary hypogonadism
Diagnostic threshold: Two or more endocrine deficiencies

items.title: Genetic Considerations in APS-2
items.content: The genetic risk for APS-2 is associated with the HLA complex on chromosome 6. Primary adrenal insufficiency in APS-2 is linked to HLA-DR3 and HLA-DR4, among other genes.
keyPoints: Genetic association: HLA complex on chromosome 6
Primary adrenal insufficiency: Strongly associated with HLA-DR3 and HLA-DR4
Other genetic associations: HLA-B8, HLA-DQ2, HLA-DQ8, MIC-A5.1 allele, PTPN22, CTLA4, IL-2Rα variants

items.title: Screening for Celiac Disease in APS-2 Patients
items.content: Celiac disease screening is crucial in APS-2 due to its association with other autoimmune conditions. The anti-tissue transglutaminase (tTg) antibody test is recommended, especially for those under 20 years old, who should be tested every 1–2 years. Positive results require confirmation and a small-bowel biopsy.
keyPoints: Screening with anti-tissue transglutaminase (tTg) antibody test
Testing frequency: every 1-2 years for those <20 years old; less frequent after age 20
Positive tTg results need confirmation and small-bowel biopsy
Asymptomatic celiac disease can lead to osteopenia, impaired growth, increased risk of gastrointestinal malignancy (especially lymphoma), and osteoporosis if untreated

items.title: Immunotherapy in Type 1 Diabetes within APS-2
items.content: Immunotherapy is primarily used for T1D in APS-2, aiming to prolong the honeymoon phase. Teplizumab, an anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody, delays clinical diabetes onset by about 3 years in stage 2 T1D patients and is approved in the U.S.
keyPoints: Immunotherapy mainly for T1D in APS-2
Therapies include modified anti-CD3 (teplizumab), rituximab, abatacept, alefacept, low-dose antithymocyte globulin, TNF-$\alpha$ inhibitors, JAK inhibitors
Teplizumab delays clinical diabetes onset by ~3 years in stage 2 T1D patients
Teplizumab is approved for use in the United States

items.subtopic: IMMUNE CHECKPOINT INHIBITOR-INDUCED ENDOCRINE AUTOIMMUNITY | IPEX
items.title: Autoimmune Endocrinopathies Induced by Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors (ICIs)
items.content: Immune checkpoint inhibitors, such as those targeting PD-1 and CTLA-4, can lead to autoimmune endocrinopathies including T1D, thyroid disease, hypophysitis, and adrenal insufficiency. These adverse events occur due to the blockade of negative immune regulation, allowing for an immune response against self-tissues.
keyPoints: Hypothyroidism occurs in ~10% of patients receiving ICIs targeting PD-1 or PD-L1.
T1D develops in 1–2% of these patients and presents with diabetic ketoacidosis, requiring lifelong insulin therapy.
Hypophysitis and adrenal insufficiency occur in <1% of treated patients.
ICIs-induced T1D has a rapid onset and is associated with HLA-DR4 (~70%) and may present with islet autoantibodies at diagnosis.
items.reference: Autoimmune Polyendocrine Syndromes · IMMUNE CHECKPOINT INHIBITOR-INDUCED ENDOCRINE AUTOIMMUNITY | IPEX

items.subtopic: IMMUNE CHECKPOINT INHIBITOR-INDUCED ENDOCRINE AUTOIMMUNITY | IPEX
items.title: Characteristics and Management of IPEX Syndrome
items.content: IPEX syndrome is a rare X-linked recessive disorder characterized by severe enteropathy, T1D, skin disease, and other autoimmune disorders. It results from mutations in the FOXP3 gene affecting regulatory T cells, leading to autoimmunity due to lack of peripheral tolerance.
keyPoints: Early onset of T1D, often at birth, is highly suggestive of IPEX (80% develop T1D).
The disease can be fatal within days but may allow survival for 12–15 years in some cases.
Treatment involves immunosuppressive therapy and hematopoietic stem cell transplantation as the only life-saving option.
Future therapies may include autologous CD4+ T cells transfected with a functioning FOXP3 gene.
items.reference: Autoimmune Polyendocrine Syndromes · IMMUNE CHECKPOINT INHIBITOR-INDUCED ENDOCRINE AUTOIMMUNITY | IPEX

items.subtopic: THYMIC TUMORS | ANTI-INSULIN RECEPTOR ANTIBODIES | INSULIN AUTOIMMUNE SYNDROME (HIRATA'S SYNDROME)
items.title: Thymic Tumors and Associated Autoimmune Diseases
items.content: Thymomas and thymic hyperplasia are linked to several autoimmune diseases, most notably myasthenia gravis (44%) and red cell aplasia (20%). These tumors may lack AIRE expression, potentially contributing to autoimmunity. Thymoma is associated with anticytokine antibodies and mucocutaneous candidiasis in adults. Most thymomas are malignant, but resection can lead to temporary remission of the autoimmune condition.
keyPoints: Myasthenia gravis is associated with thymic tumors in 44% of cases.
Red cell aplasia occurs in 20% of patients with thymic tumors.
Thymomas often lack AIRE expression, contributing to autoimmunity.
Thymoma can lead to anticytokine antibodies and mucocutaneous candidiasis.
Most thymomas are malignant; resection may cause temporary autoimmune remission.
items.reference: Autoimmune Polyendocrine Syndromes · THYMIC TUMORS | ANTI-INSULIN RECEPTOR ANTIBODIES | INSULIN AUTOIMMUNE SYNDROME (HIRATA'S SYNDROME)

items.subtopic: THYMIC TUMORS | ANTI-INSULIN RECEPTOR ANTIBODIES | INSULIN AUTOIMMUNE SYNDROME (HIRATA'S SYNDROME)
items.title: Anti-Insulin Receptor Antibodies and Insulin Resistance
items.content: Anti-insulin receptor antibodies cause severe insulin resistance, requiring >100,000 units of insulin daily for partial hyperglycemia control. Patients may present with acanthosis nigricans and associated autoimmune illnesses like lupus or Sjögren's syndrome. The disease course is variable, with spontaneous remissions possible. B lymphocyte-targeting therapies (rituximab, cyclophosphamide, pulse steroids) have been effective in inducing remission.
keyPoints: Anti-insulin receptor antibodies cause severe insulin resistance.
>100,000 units of insulin may be needed daily for partial control.
Acanthosis nigricans and autoimmune illnesses like lupus or Sjögren's syndrome are common.
Disease course is variable; spontaneous remissions occur.
B lymphocyte-targeting therapies can induce remission.
items.reference: Autoimmune Polyendocrine Syndromes · THYMIC TUMORS | ANTI-INSULIN RECEPTOR ANTIBODIES | INSULIN AUTOIMMUNE SYNDROME (HIRATA'S SYNDROME)

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of POEMS Syndrome
items.content: POEMS syndrome is characterized by polyneuropathy, organomegaly, endocrinopathy, M-protein, and skin changes. It often presents with diabetes mellitus (50%), primary gonadal failure (70%), and plasma cell dyscrasia with sclerotic bony lesions. Diagnosis involves recognizing these features along with associated findings like hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy, and hyperpigmentation.
keyPoints: POEMS syndrome presents in the fifth to sixth decade of life.
Median survival after diagnosis is <3 years.
Treatments include thalidomide, lenalidomide, radiotherapy for localized plasma cell lesions, chemotherapy, dexamethasone, and autologous stem cell transplantation.
items.reference: Autoimmune Polyendocrine Syndromes · POEMS SYNDROME | OTHER DISORDERS

items.title: Endocrine Manifestations in POEMS Syndrome
items.content: Patients with POEMS syndrome often exhibit endocrinopathies such as diabetes mellitus and primary gonadal failure. The hypogonadism is due to primary gonadal disease, evidenced by elevated plasma levels of follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone.
keyPoints: Diabetes mellitus occurs in 50% of patients.
Primary gonadal failure occurs in 70% of patients.
Hypoglycemia responds to small, subcutaneous doses of insulin.
items.reference: Autoimmune Polyendocrine Syndromes · POEMS SYNDROME | OTHER DISORDERS

items.title: Pathophysiology and Treatment Targets in POEMS Syndrome
items.content: The syndrome is associated with elevated levels of vascular endothelial growth factor, IL-1 $  eta $, IL-6, and tumor necrosis factor $  eta $. Treatments like thalidomide and lenalidomide decrease these factors. Understanding the role of cytokines aids in targeting treatment strategies.
keyPoints: Elevated levels of vascular endothelial growth factor and inflammatory cytokines are present.
Therapies include thalidomide and lenalidomide, which reduce vascular endothelial growth factor.
items.reference: Autoimmune Polyendocrine Syndromes · POEMS SYNDROME | OTHER DISORDERS

items.title: Importance of Central Laboratories and Tertiary Care for APS Diagnosis
items.content: Diagnosis of Autoimmune Polyendocrine Syndromes (APS) necessitates specialized laboratory capabilities to detect unique autoantibodies and perform gene sequencing. Early recognition and referral to tertiary care centers are crucial for confirming diagnosis and initiating treatment, which can significantly improve patient outcomes.
keyPoints: Access to central laboratories with specific testing capabilities is essential.
Timely referral and consultation with tertiary care centers are important for early diagnosis and treatment initiation.

items.title: Genetic Prevalence of AIRE Mutations in APS-1 Across Populations
items.content: AIRE recessive gene mutations, which cause APS-1, are prevalent in certain populations such as Finns, Iranian Jews, Sardinians, Norwegians, and Irish. Awareness of these genetic frequencies can increase suspicion for APS-1 in patients from these backgrounds.
keyPoints: AIRE recessive gene mutations are common in specific populations: Finns, Iranian Jews, Sardinians, Norwegians, and Irish.
Recognition of population-specific mutation frequency aids in raising clinical suspicion.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a type of autoimmune polyendocrine syndrome (APS) mentioned in the passage?
items.explanation: The passage mentions APS-1, APS-2, APS-3, and APS-4 but not APS-5.

items.question: What is the primary reason given for not further subdividing APS-2 into additional types?
options: It does not clarify understanding of disease pathogenesis or prevention of complications.
items.explanation: The passage states that further subdivision of APS-2 into additional types does not clarify our understanding of disease pathogenesis or prevention of complications in individual patients.
items.reference: Autoimmune Polyendocrine Syndromes · Autoimmune Polyendocrine Syndromes

items.question: What is suggested by the presence of nonendocrine disease associations in autoimmune polyendocrine syndromes?
options: The underlying autoimmune disorder exclusively involves endocrine targets.
Nonendocrine tissues are not affected by these disorders.
The autoimmune disorder predominantly involves endocrine targets but can also affect other tissues.
Endocrine and nonendocrine diseases are unrelated.
items.explanation: The passage suggests that although the underlying autoimmune disorder predominantly involves endocrine targets, it does not exclude other tissues from being affected.
items.reference: Autoimmune Polyendocrine Syndromes · Autoimmune Polyendocrine Syndromes

items.question: Which gene mutation is primarily responsible for the classical form of APS-1?
options: CARD region mutations in AIRE gene
PHD1 domain mutations in AIRE gene
items.explanation: The classical form of APS-1 is caused by autosomal recessive mutations primarily found in the CARD region of the AIRE gene.

items.question: What is a common early manifestation of APS-1?
items.explanation: Chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis is often the first manifestation of classical APS-1, typically appearing in infancy.

items.question: Which autoantibodies are associated with chronic oral candidiasis in APS-1?
items.explanation: Chronic oral candidiasis in APS-1 is associated with anticytokine autoantibodies against IL-17A, IL-17F, and IL-22.

items.question: What diagnostic marker can identify nearly all cases of APS-1?
items.explanation: The detection of anti-interferon alpha and omega antibodies can identify nearly all cases with APS-1.

items.question: Which autoantibody would confirm the diagnosis of mucocutaneous candidiasis in a patient with APS-1?
options: Anti-cytokine antibodies to IL-17 and IL-22
items.explanation: Detection of anticytokine antibodies to IL-17 and IL-22 confirms mucocutaneous candidiasis in APS-1.

items.question: What should be screened for regularly in patients with APS-1?
options: Anti-cytokine antibodies to IL-17 and IL-22 only
Autoantibodies related to type 1 diabetes, pernicious anemia, and other component conditions
items.explanation: Regular screening for autoantibodies related to type 1 diabetes, pernicious anemia, and other component conditions is recommended.

items.question: What laboratory finding indicates the need for a subsequent ultrasound of the spleen in APS-1 patients?
items.explanation: The presence of Howell-Jolly bodies indicates the need for an ultrasound of the spleen.

items.question: Which HLA alleles are strongly associated with primary adrenal insufficiency in APS-2?
items.explanation: Primary adrenal insufficiency in APS-2 is strongly associated with both HLA-DR3 and HLA-DR4.

items.question: What is the prevalence of APS-2?
items.explanation: APS-2 has a prevalence of 1–2 in 100,000.

items.question: Which autoimmune condition is NOT frequently associated with APS-2?
items.explanation: Rheumatoid arthritis is not frequently associated with APS-2.

items.question: Which autoantibody is highly diagnostic for risk of adrenal insufficiency in APS-2?
items.explanation: Antibodies to 21-hydroxylase are highly diagnostic for risk of adrenal insufficiency.

items.question: Which antibody test is recommended for screening celiac disease in individuals under 20 years of age?
items.explanation: The anti-tissue transglutaminase (tTg) antibody test is used for screening celiac disease, especially in those under 20 years of age.

items.question: What should follow a positive anti-tissue transglutaminase (tTg) antibody test result?
items.explanation: A positive tTg antibody test should be confirmed on repeat testing, followed by a small-bowel biopsy to document pathologic changes of celiac disease.

items.question: Which immunotherapy has been approved in the United States for delaying the onset of clinical diabetes in stage 2 T1D?
items.explanation: The anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody teplizumab is approved for delaying the onset of clinical diabetes in stage 2 T1D.

items.statement: APS-1 and APS-2 are the only classifications of autoimmune polyendocrine syndromes.

items.statement: Understanding autoimmune polyendocrine syndromes can lead to early diagnosis and treatment of additional disorders in patients and their family members.

items.reference: Autoimmune Polyendocrine Syndromes · Autoimmune Polyendocrine Syndromes

items.statement: Gonadal failure affects men more frequently than women in APS-1.
items.explanation: Gonadal failure occurs more frequently in women (70%) compared to men (25%) in APS-1.

items.statement: The diagnosis of APS-1 can be confirmed by identifying mutations in the AIRE gene.
items.explanation: Genetic analysis of the AIRE gene should be undertaken to identify mutations for diagnosing APS-1.

items.statement: Replacement therapy with thyroid hormone can precipitate an adrenal crisis in patients with undiagnosed adrenal insufficiency.
items.explanation: Thyroid hormone replacement can precipitate an adrenal crisis if adrenal insufficiency is not diagnosed and treated first.

items.statement: Ketoconazole treatment for mucocutaneous candidiasis in individuals with subclinical adrenal insufficiency does not pose any risk.
items.explanation: Treatment with ketoconazole may precipitate an adrenal crisis in those with subclinical adrenal insufficiency.

items.statement: The onset of APS-2 often occurs in childhood.
items.explanation: APS-2 often has its onset in adulthood, with a peak incidence between 20 and 60 years of age.

items.statement: The '5.1' allele of MIC-A is strongly associated with Addison's disease.
items.explanation: The MIC-A5.1 allele has a very strong association with Addison's disease.

items.statement: Hypocalcemia in APS-2 patients is more likely due to hypoparathyroidism than malabsorption.
items.explanation: In APS-2, hypocalcemia is more likely due to malabsorption from undiagnosed celiac disease rather than hypoparathyroidism.

items.statement: Immunotherapy for autoimmune endocrine diseases has been widely successful in achieving long-term outcomes.
items.explanation: Although immunotherapies can prolong the honeymoon phase of T1D, none have achieved long-term success.

items.assertion: The classical form of APS-1 is an autosomal recessive disorder.
items.reason: It is caused by mutations in the AIRE gene, primarily found in the CARD region.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Addison's disease is found in nearly all cases of APS-1.
items.reason: It is one of the three major components of the disorder.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because Addison's disease is found in nearly 80% of cases, not nearly all.

items.assertion: Screening for islet autoantibodies can identify individuals with preclinical T1D.

items.subtopic: THYMIC TUMORS | ANTI-INSULIN RECEPTOR ANTIBODIES | INSULIN AUTOIMMUNE SYNDROME (HIRATA'S SYNDROME)
items.assertion: Discontinuation of methimazole therapy generally leads to resolution of insulin autoimmune syndrome.
items.reason: The syndrome is associated with methimazole therapy and tends to resolve over time after discontinuing the medication.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why discontinuation of methimazole leads to resolution.
items.reference: Autoimmune Polyendocrine Syndromes · THYMIC TUMORS | ANTI-INSULIN RECEPTOR ANTIBODIES | INSULIN AUTOIMMUNE SYNDROME (HIRATA'S SYNDROME)

items.assertion: Wolfram's syndrome is a slowly progressive neurodegenerative process.
items.reason: It involves nonautoimmune selective destruction of pancreatic beta cells.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: Autoimmune Polyendocrine Syndromes · POEMS SYNDROME | OTHER DISORDERS

items.assertion: Patients with Down's syndrome should be regularly screened for autoimmune diseases.
items.reason: Down's syndrome is associated with an increased risk of developing T1D, thyroiditis, and celiac disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
